skill.name: matplotlib
skill.description: Create and customize Matplotlib visualizations with guidance for plot types, styling, layouts, exports, troubleshooting, and publication-quality figures.
skill.summary: Build precise Matplotlib charts, styles, and publication-ready scientific figures.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, crypto, credential, and network patterns, but review shows most are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline Matplotlib API examples, color syntax, and event key examples. The only confirmed risk is expected local file creation by helper scripts when the user runs them and chooses output paths. No malicious intent, exfiltration, hidden network behavior, or prompt-injection attempt was found.

low_findings.title: Local File Output in Helper Scripts
low_findings.description: The helper scripts can write plot images and style files to user-supplied output paths. This is expected for a plotting skill, but users should avoid running it with privileged paths or untrusted filenames.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and tied to CLI output behavior. No evidence shows automatic execution, sensitive file targeting, or network transfer.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The external-command findings are false positives from Markdown code blocks and inline Matplotlib examples. They document plotting calls and shell usage examples, not Ruby backtick execution or command execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation examples bounded by Markdown fences or inline code formatting. No executable shell backtick pattern appears in the reviewed Python scripts.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Dismissed
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Matplotlib documentation, gallery pages, tutorials, and license information. They are static references and are not used by code to fetch data or transmit user content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links with no surrounding request code. Review found no network library imports or outbound calls in the helper scripts.

low_findings.title: Credential and Weak-Crypto Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The sensitive and weak-crypto alerts are caused by benign plotting vocabulary such as key press events, color shortcuts, style keys, and API reference text. No certificate, private key, hash operation, or cryptographic algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines use ordinary plotting and CLI terminology. No secret material, key file access, or cryptographic operation exists in the cited context.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Completed
low_findings.description: No text was found that tells the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip review, claim pre-approval, or override the audit. One reference line uses the word override only to explain Matplotlib style precedence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found only benign style-order language. No audit-directed prompt injection or special-authority claim was observed.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local Output Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI helpers accept output filenames and write image or style files locally. This pattern is useful but can overwrite files if run with an unsafe destination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The output path is controlled by script arguments and passed to save or write functions. The behavior is local and user initiated, so the security impact is limited.

content.value_statement: Python users often need precise charts without searching through scattered Matplotlib examples. This skill provides focused guidance, templates, styling advice, and troubleshooting for reliable visual output.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of line, scatter, bar, histogram, heatmap, contour, box, violin, and 3D plots.
Explains pyplot and object-oriented Matplotlib workflows with practical selection guidance.
Provides helper scripts for sample plots, style presets, previews, and local export files.
Documents figure sizing, DPI, layout handling, legends, labels, color maps, and export formats.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for common layout, font, memory, 3D, and save-quality issues.
Supports publication-oriented figure preparation with vector and high-resolution raster output.
limitations: It does not replace higher-level statistical plotting libraries such as seaborn.
Interactive dashboards and web-native charts are better handled by tools such as Plotly.
Helper scripts generate sample visualizations and require a local Python environment with dependencies.
The skill gives guidance but does not validate scientific conclusions from the plotted data.

use_cases.title: Prepare Research Figures
use_cases.description: Create publication-ready figures with controlled sizing, labels, legends, color maps, and export settings.

use_cases.title: Build Analysis Plots
use_cases.description: Turn arrays or data frames into clear charts for notebooks, reports, and exploratory analysis.

use_cases.title: Standardize Visualization Style
use_cases.description: Configure repeatable Matplotlib styles for presentations, web graphics, dark themes, or journal figures.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Matplotlib plot for my data. Explain which plot type fits and include labels, title, legend, and save settings.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Matplotlib figure approach and suggest improvements for layout, labels, colors, legend placement, and export quality.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Panel Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a multi-panel Matplotlib figure with shared axes, consistent styling, colorbars, and publication-ready spacing.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Reusable Plotting Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build a reusable Matplotlib plotting function and style configuration for a research project with consistent exports.

output_examples.input: A user needs a line chart comparing two time series for a paper.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends an object-oriented figure, clear axis labels, a compact legend, subtle grid lines, and a high-resolution or vector export.

output_examples.input: A user has overlapping subplot labels in a dense scientific figure.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests constrained layout, adjusted figure size, shared axes where useful, and targeted spacing changes before export.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent visuals for reports and presentations.
output_examples.output: The skill helps choose a style preset, preview the result, and save a reusable Matplotlib style file for repeated use.

best_practices: Use the object-oriented Figure and Axes interface for reusable or complex plots.
Set figure size, DPI, labels, legends, and layout before exporting final figures.
Choose color maps that match the data type and remain readable for colorblind users.
anti_patterns: Relying on implicit pyplot state across complex multi-panel figures.
Using rainbow color maps for ordered quantitative data without a strong reason.
Saving final figures without checking layout, text overlap, resolution, and file format.

faq.question: When should I use this skill instead of seaborn?
faq.answer: Use this skill when you need detailed control over Matplotlib figures. Use seaborn for quick statistical plots with built-in themes.

faq.question: Can it help with publication-ready figures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers DPI, vector formats, labels, legends, layouts, and style choices used for papers and technical reports.

faq.question: Does it create interactive dashboards?
faq.answer: No. It can explain Matplotlib interactivity and animations, but dashboard work is usually better with web-focused tools.

faq.question: What files are included?
faq.answer: The skill includes main guidance, reference documents, a plot template script, and a style configurator script.

faq.question: Are the helper scripts required?
faq.answer: No. They are optional examples. You can use the documentation without running any script.

faq.question: What should I check before exporting a final chart?
faq.answer: Check labels, legends, color contrast, layout spacing, figure size, DPI, and whether raster or vector output is best.
